speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today's date is February 6, 1995. Survivor being interviewed is Marion Lazan. The interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in Hewlett, New York in the United States of America, and the language is English. Mrs. Lazan, tell me as much as you can of your childhood. I know you were very young, only about four years old, when your time in your original city ended and the war began. So maybe some of your memories will be family stories, but tell me as much as you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: From what I know, life for us in Germany was very much as it is here for people today. I lived with my parents, my two-year-older brother, and my-- with my grandparents in Hoya, which is a little town near Hanover. My grandparents were in a shoe business, and we lived very comfortably. And we-- life was very easy for us. We had-- the shoe business was very successful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was in the early-- mid-30s, actually, when things became increasingly less comfortable for us Jews. In fact, for my brother, he was born in 1932, I recall being told that even in his carriage stones were thrown by children and passerbys. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My grandparents were in their late 70s and were ailing. And even though things were becoming increasingly more difficult in Germany and my parents had felt that it was time to prepare to emigrate, to leave Germany, my grandparents just didn't feel the urgency of the matter and didn't want to leave. And we, of course, could not leave them. Then in 1938, within 11 days of each other, my grandparents passed away. We then immediately made arrangements to emigrate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then in 1938, Kristallnacht, November of '38. That was the beginnings of the pogrom against the Jews of Germany. And were-- it was major destructions of synagogues, businesses, Jewish establishments in general. And it was at that time that my father was taken from us and sent to a concentration camp, Buchenwald. And it was only because our papers, immigration papers, were ready that my father was released after only 10 days incarceration. It was a very frightening time for my mother, in particular, because when my father was taken away we had no idea where he was and-- or whether we would ever see him again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but then when he was released, we continued with our preparations to emigrate. And we left Hoya and went to Hanover. And in Hanover, we were there for just a few months and then left for Holland from where we were to sail to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before we continue in Holland--

speaker: interviewer
text: --I wonder if we could go back a little bit in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the family business, what was it like, and any stories that you remember from your parents and grandparents about Hoya? What was the town like? What-- what kind of family did you have there? What were some of the family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hoya is a very small town. In fact, my-- my mother lives here in Queens, Far Rockaway, and the buildings surrounding where my mother lives now have more people in it than the little town of Hoya had. There were maybe 3,000 or 4,000 people is it. And my father was one of six children, and his siblings-- some of his siblings also lived with us in Hoya in the same house. I was told that we had our own apartment. My grandparents lived in one apartment, we-- on one level of the house, and we lived in another level, and then the store was downstairs. And a very well-established store and a very well thought of business. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as far as holidays, we were as observant as we possibly could be in a small town such as Hoya. There was a synagogue and-- and maybe, I believe, seven or eight Jewish families is-- is all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's about all that I believe is--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the business like? What did you sell exactly and--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a shoe business, men, women, and children shoes, as well as clothing. And it, I think, was one of the largest shoe and clothing stores in the little town of Hoya. And business was good, until the Germans took over, and we were forced to sell our home and our business for very little money. And, of course, then everything was lost. And we then, by way of Hanover, went to Holland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In Holland, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents were asked to settle in Gouda. That's Gouda. And there they were assigned to take care of some 125 children whose parents sent them from various parts of Europe-- Europe to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] save them from the Nazis. And my parents took care of these children. And that was from the beginning of '39 until-- about April of '39, I believe, until the end of '39. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shortly thereafter, we were to-- actually, our plans and our papers were all ready to immigrate to-- to America, and that was to be in May of 1940. Our number was constantly set back, and this was, I'm sorry to say, due to the American State Department. We were always set back. We were supposed to leave in-- in May, and then it was set back another week or two and then more and more set back, until finally the Germans invaded Holland. And then it was too late for us to leave, and we were trapped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the interim, of course, all of our belongings, which were on board ship in Rotterdam, were completely destroyed as the harbor of Rotterdam was bombed by the Germans. I, at the age of four, had my trousseau packed already, because we, as I said, we did have the moneys in Germany. And we had lifts packed. Lifts are huge containers. And we had our furniture and linens and things of that that we were allowed to take out of Germany at that time, and everything was completely destroyed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we were sent to Camp Westerbork in Holland, which is a transit camp where people from throughout Europe, and particularly from Holland, were brought. Many of them were taken from their hiding places, and thousands of people were sent to Westerbork. Westerbork was tolerable as a transit camp. And we lived in barracks, but we were allowed to stay together as a family. And life for us children was very [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] plain. We had nothing to do. It was stagnant. And-- but we did have enough food to eat. We ate in a communal dining room. And as I said, we had like a-- a one-bedroom little apartment within the barracks. So life was bearable. And later on, though, as time went on, many, many more people came to Westerbork, and we had to share our apartment, which was fine. As far as food, it was simple and plain, but sufficient. And I remember craving sweets, and we-- somebody who had come to us had brought [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] saccharine. And I figured, well, saccharine's got to be specially good. So I took a handful of saccharine and thought I would satisfy my craving of sweets. It was not nearly as pleasant as I had hoped it would be, to say the least. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were in Westerbork until-- really from the very beginning of 1940 until January of 1944. It was at that time that people were sent from Westerbork to various concentration camps in Germany and Eastern Europe. And we children really welcomed the change. We were excited. We were naive, and we were excited that there was going to be a change in our lives and hoping, of course, for the better. And the adults and parents somehow realized that things were going to get a lot worse for us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in February of 1944 that it was our turn to be shipped out of Westerbork. We were allowed to take [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just a knapsack a person, and whatever we could fit into the knapsack that-- those were our belongings, and that was it. We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had the privilege of being sent to Bergen-Belsen as opposed to Auschwitz, because arrangements were made through my mother's sister, who lived in Palestine at the time, for us to be exchanged for a group of German religious sect-- I think it was called the Templars-- who were in Palestine, and we were to be exchanged for them. She made arrangements, and we would be among the few hundred of those. Initially, there were 1,000 or so, but then it was cut back to a few hundred, and we were to be among that group to be exchanged. And therefore, we were a privileged-- within that privileged group to be sent to Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As I said, in February we were walked to the station of Westerbork. And as we saw the trains, cattle cars, our fears began to mount. And we realized that our hopes, my hopes as a child-- at that time I was nine years old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that things were not going to be as easy or as comfortable as we had hoped it would be. We [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got on the train. And it was-- I don't remember how-- how long a-- a trip it was, but it was winter. As I said, it was February. It was bitter cold when we arrived in-- at our destination, Bergen-Belsen. And there we were met by the Gestapo, the SS. I remember them with their high black boots, rifles by their sides, and German shepherds. These German shepherds were trained to attack, I learned later, only people in civilian clothing. And the SS had a good time threatening us with their rifles and with their dogs. I was just nine years old, cold, frightened, and no taller than the height of-- of-- of the-- the boots that the Germans wore, the SS wore. And my brother was always very protective of me, and he would stand by my side. And we-- I was always very grateful that he was there for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we walked from the station of Bergen-Belsen to the camp itself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There-- ah, I need a tissue. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you. We-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the-- the barracks, well, the camp itself was surrounded by electrified barbed wire, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were high guard houses in various parts of-- surrounding the-- the camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whenever it got dark at night, there were searchlights from the guard houses constantly roaming the camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very frightening, overwhelming situation. The barracks were built initially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the First World War in 1917, 1918, meant for just 100 soldiers at the time. We were with 600 in the barracks. There were triple-decker beds, bunks, with two people in one bunk. I was very fortunate that I stayed with my mother, and my brother was able to stay with my father. Men and women were separated. We were fortunate in that we were able to get glimpses of one another every so often. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our bunks were made up of three or four slats, wooden slats, some straw as a mattress, and one thin blanket. And that was for the two of us. That was our-- those were our quarters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- our food consisted of a pat of butter, a slice of bread a day. And then we would get, every once in a while, a turnip soup with some gristle in it. The bread and the butter was later cut back to just once a week, and that was given to us only if our quarters were in order, if our blanket was straightened. There was constant inspection, constant fear that things might not be right, might not be the way it suits the Germans. So it always had to be on guard to make sure that our quarters were in order. We would give each other, on our birthdays, the slice of bread that we would have saved-- that we saved from previous weeks. And not that we had a calendar there, but we had an approximate idea as to what the calendar months were. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember at one point seeing a cart with what I thought was firewood for the one small oven that we had in our barrack. And actually, what I saw being hauled away were naked bodies, one stacked on top of the other. And it was sights like this, the constant death and filth and horror that we lived with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every day, without fail, we were asked to stand on appell. That meant that we were all sent out into a large field, and we were lined up five in a row, and there we were counted. And everyone had to be accounted for before we were allowed to leave. This was no matter what the weather, no matter how warm or how cold, how rainy or how cold, how much snow or ice there was. We had to stand at attention and until everyone was accounted for. The Germans did their utmost to break us mentally, physically, and emotionally. And it-- it was not unusual for people to try to escape, even though they knew very well that it was next to impossible to succeed. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we would realize their failure at the attempt when we saw their bodies electrocuted against the wires. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I, personally, spent my days with a game of superstition. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would say to myself, if I were to find-- I would constantly search, and I would search for four pebbles, four perfect pebbles approximately the same size and shape. And I said to myself, if I find those four pebbles, that that-- that would mean that my father, my mother, my brother, and I would survive. It was a torturous pastime. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a game that I-- I played, I needed to play, because it gave me some distant hope, some-- something to hold onto. And this went on daily. And whenever I would start to search for my pebbles, I would make it my business, I would-- to find that fourth pebble. It may not always have been exactly the size or the shape as the first two or three. But I rationalized, and I felt that this is the best I could do here. It'll be fine. We will all survive. I have my four pebbles. And it gave me the little extra strength and extra hope. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We, as I said, we went through some bitter cold days, and it was not unusual to-- to have frostbites. And of course, there was nothing that-- we had nothing to care for, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no medication, no nothing to care for our frostbitten fingers or toes. And we used to treat them with the warmth of our own urine. That's all that we had to soothe the discomfort with. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once a week, we were sent out-- taken to an area to shower. We had heard of extermination camps. We had heard of Auschwitz, and we knew of the gas chambers there. And we were told that we were just to go to the showers and there, under the watchful eyes of the Germans, we were told to undress, and we were sent into the showers. And of course, we never knew when those faucets were turned on whether it would be gas that would come out or water. So there, again, the Germans did everything in their power to ridiculous us, to torment us, and it was just another way. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It was impossible to keep oneself clean. We were covered with lice-- hair lice, clothes lice. There we learned that there was a distinct difference between hair lice and clothes lice. We spent some of our free time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] killing the gnats, the-- the little eggs between our nails, and it would make a clicking sound. This was part of our-- the children's pastime, and that's what we did. And they were the-- the eggs were particularly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] plentiful in-- in the seams of clothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Toilets and washing facilities were blocks away in the most primitive outhouses. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Toilets were in the long benches with holes next to one another. There was no such thing as toilet paper. We had no toothpaste. We had no toothbrushes. There was no soap. There was nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Near the latrines, near those outhouses, near the toilets, was long pipes with trickles of-- trickles of water coming out. That's how we had to wash ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Life was getting increasingly more difficult. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the filth and the-- the constant death surrounding us was just overwhelming. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was teenagers and men who were the first to die. And it was women, and mothers in particular, who survived. And mothers lived-- they had to live to save and to take care of their children and make sure that they would pull through. And it was the inner strength of my mother that really saw us through. She is a remarkable lady who has enormous inner strength. And there is no doubt in my mind that it is her strong will that saw us through. My mother worked in a kitchen, and my father worked in a shoe factory, where she-- where he pulled shoes apart and boots apart. And my mother was able to smuggle [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some potatoes and a little can to our bunk one day. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the little strips of-- from our slats, she was able to make a little fire, and somehow she was able to collect a little water. And she was able to get salt, and she was able to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make a little soup for us. This was done in very private on our bed, on our bunk, and I was on the bunk with her hiding the set-up. It took an hour or two before things start to boil, before the soup started to cook, when the Germans came into the barrack for their routine inspection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In order to hide the set-up, the soup spilled, and it had been boiling already for a short time. It spilled on my leg. We learned self-discipline the hard way. And I knew that if I were to cry out, it would cost us our lives. I was 10 years old at the time, and I, with the soup on my leg, kept very quiet and had a severe, severe burn-- my upper leg, my lower leg. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was towards the end of our stay in Bergen-Belsen. This was in-- already in end of March of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we'll-- we'll continue on the next.
